title: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States lays out five operational principles for Christian abolitionist candidates running in Republican primaries: outwork the opposition, draw sharp contrast with pro-life rhetoric, refuse to be a single-issue candidate while keeping abortion central, never agree to compromised legislation, and conduct the entire campaign as service to Christ.
audiences: Prospective abolitionist candidate weighing a primary run
Pro-life voter being asked to distinguish abolition from pro-lifeism
Pastor or Christian leader evaluating political engagement
Pro-life establishment operative defending the status quo

questions.question: Why does the article insist that abolitionist candidates must outwork their opponents rather than relying on funding parity?
questions.audience: Prospective abolitionist candidate weighing a primary run
questions.answer: Because the abolitionist movement does not yet have the financial or institutional muscle of the pro-life establishment, candidates will almost always be outspent and out-endorsed; the only way to overcome that gap is sheer volume of voter contact through calls, door-knocking, mailers, ads, signs, and local Republican event presence.
questions.quote: The Abolitionist Movement is rapidly growing in size and organization, but we do not have the resources of the Pro-Life Establishment. Especially if you are challenging an incumbent, your opponent will likely have more money than you, and will certainly have more political connections.

questions.question: What concrete examples does the article cite to prove that an outworked, underfunded abolitionist can still beat an incumbent?
questions.answer: The 2020 races of Warren Hamilton in Oklahoma SD7 (outspent more than four-to-one against Larry Boggs, who was backed by the governor, both U.S. Senators, and nearly half the State Senate) and John Jacob in Indiana HD93 (outspent more than two-to-one against Dollyne Sherman, endorsed by the governor, Indiana Right to Life, the NRA, and the Indiana Chamber of Commerce). Both candidates won their Republican primaries and the general election by aggressive door-to-door work and coalition-building with liberty-minded organizations.
questions.quote: Hamilton and organizations supporting him were out-spent by Boggs and Boggs-supporting organizations more than four-to-one.

questions.question: How should an abolitionist candidate's messaging differ from the standard pro-life vocabulary?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter being asked to distinguish abolition from pro-lifeism
questions.answer: Candidates should use sharp, specific phrases like 'Abolish Abortion,' 'Equal Protection for Preborn Children,' and 'End Abortion Now' while explicitly avoiding vague terms like 'pro-life,' 'I support pro-life bills,' or 'the life issue,' because those phrases are interchangeable with the language used by the politicians who keep abortion legal and therefore destroy the contrast voters need in order to choose.
questions.quote: Avoid using phrases that make you blend in with your opponent and with the establishment that is keeping abortion legal.

questions.question: Why does the author argue that contrast is more important in an abolitionist primary campaign than in ordinary races?
questions.answer: Because pro-life slogans are deliberately vague tools that incumbents use to disguise their compromise; without explicit contrast, an abolitionist will be lumped in with the very politicians keeping abortion legal. Drawing the line publicly is not just a campaign tactic, it is the same line of demarcation the movement uses to convert pro-lifers into abolitionists.
questions.quote: Pro-life political messaging is a cesspool of meaningless generalities which serve to delay the abolition of abortion. Abolitionist candidates should not try to blend in with this cesspool. They should rise above it and expose the cesspool for what it is.

questions.question: What five points does the article say should anchor an abolitionist candidate's platform?
questions.audience: Prospective abolitionist candidate weighing a primary run
questions.answer: The wickedness of the status quo, the failure of compromising pro-life politicians, that abortion must be abolished and not regulated, interposition against Roe v. Wade, and equal protection for preborn children. The article supplies a sample stump statement built around these five points that candidates are free to adapt or reuse wholesale.
questions.quote:
1. The wickedness of the status quo
2. The failure of compromising pro-life politicians
3. Abortion must be abolished, not regulated
4. Interposition against *Roe v. Wade*
5. Equal protection for preborn children

questions.question: Does the article tell candidates to run as single-issue abortion candidates?
questions.audience: Prospective abolitionist candidate weighing a primary run
questions.answer: No. Silberman explicitly says candidates should articulate just positions on school choice, economic freedom, family rights, gender sanity, free speech, and the Second Amendment, but he insists that none of those issues, even taken together, equal the moral weight of the abortion holocaust, so abolition must remain prominently featured rather than buried alongside other planks.
questions.quote: You should address many topics well, but abolishing abortion should feature prominently in your campaign.

questions.question: How should an abolitionist candidate handle questionnaires from pro-life establishment organizations?
questions.audience: Prospective abolitionist candidate weighing a primary run
questions.answer: Refuse to endorse compromise. Specifically, the article recommends ignoring questions that ask candidates to support pro-life bills which compromise with abortion, or ignoring the questionnaire entirely. The candidate should not provoke pro-life groups unnecessarily, but if those groups intervene against the campaign, the candidate must publicly expose them as frauds working to keep abortion legal.
questions.quote: Most of them will ask whether you support pro-life bills which compromise with abortion. Do not agree to support these bills. We recommend that you ignore those particular questions or ignore the whole questionnaire.

questions.question: What is the theological basis the article gives for why politics cannot be a 'sacred vs. secular' carve-out?
questions.audience: Pastor or Christian leader evaluating political engagement
questions.answer: Silberman argues that treating politics as a separate amoral sphere of pure power-negotiation is the doorway to compromise and is itself a denial of the fifth tenet of abolitionism. He grounds the rejection in 1 Corinthians 10:31 and Abraham Kuyper's claim of Christ's sovereignty over every square inch of human existence.
questions.quote: There is not a square inch in the whole domain of our human existence over which Christ, who is Sovereign over all, does not cry, 'Mine!'

questions.question: How does the article respond to political consultants who advise 'noncommittal, timid, status quo' messaging?
questions.answer: It dismisses that advice as outdated. Silberman says today's Republican primary voters have rejected wishy-washy lip service and are actively yearning for candidates who will speak strongly and clearly to important issues, including a clear demand for the abolition of abortion rather than pro-life triangulation.
questions.quote: Today's Republican voters have no stomach for milquetoast smooth talkers. They are yearning for candidates who will speak clearly and strongly to important issues. They want abortion abolished. Not wishy-washy pro-life lip service.

questions.question: Does honoring Christ in a campaign exclude strategic thinking and tactical flexibility?
questions.audience: Pastor or Christian leader evaluating political engagement
questions.answer: No. The article makes room for discernment, efficiency, and varied tactics, including times to push, times to pull, times for direct confrontation, and times for wise maneuvering. What it forbids is any framework that severs prophetic witness and gospel preeminence from political work, which it calls both unbiblical and ultimately ineffective.
questions.quote: This is not to say there isn't room for strategic thinking. We should strive for effectiveness and efficiency. There are times to push and times to pull; times for direct confrontation and times for wise maneuvering; times for the hard truth and times for an encouraging word.

questions.question: What sample platform language does the article offer that candidates can adopt verbatim?
questions.audience: Prospective abolitionist candidate weighing a primary run
questions.answer: Silberman supplies a model paragraph framing abortion as state-sanctioned crime against humanity, repudiating do-nothing pro-life legislation, pledging immediate abolition legislation with equal protection, invoking love-of-neighbor, calling for an end to compromise with evil, and rejecting submission to Roe v. Wade. Candidates are explicitly invited to use any part or the whole of it.
questions.quote: More than 5,000 babies are murdered in Oklahoma every year with the approval of Oklahoma State law. This is a crime against humanity and I am not running for office so that I can pay lip service to the issue and pass do-nothing legislation like most pro-life politicians. I am running to establish justice for preborn children.

questions.question: Who does the article recommend candidates contact for practical campaign mentoring beyond abolitionist messaging?
questions.audience: Prospective abolitionist candidate weighing a primary run
questions.answer: Former Oklahoma State Senator Joseph Silk at the Liberty Rising Institute, and Tim Gillespie, a pastor and founder of the Oklahoma Second Amendment Association, are named as resources for the general mechanics of running for office that the article itself does not cover.
questions.quote: we recommend [contacting former State Senator Joseph Silk at Liberty Rising Institute](https://www.liberty-rising.org/#contact) and Tim Gillespie, Pastor and Oklahoma Second Amendment Association Founder (timgillespieok@gmail.com).

related.title: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
related.reason: Case study of one of the two flagship 2020 victories cited as proof that outworking and out-contrasting an establishment-backed incumbent can win a Republican primary.

related.title: Warren Hamilton Defeats Larry Boggs, Margie Alfonso Defeats Clay Iiams
related.reason: Direct reporting on the Oklahoma SD7 race that Silberman uses as the article's central illustration of an abolitionist beating a four-to-one funding gap.

related.title: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
related.reason: Demonstrates Principle 4 in action: the pro-life establishment opposes abolitionist candidates, and the candidate must publicly expose them rather than capitulate.

related.title: Larry Boggs Campaigns With 2019 & 2020 Abortion Rights Men of the Year
related.reason: Concrete example of the contrast-drawing tactic recommended in Principle 2: naming a pro-life incumbent's actual record rather than accepting his slogans.

related.title: Here's What We're Asking Oklahoma Candidates
related.reason: Counterpart resource showing the abolitionist questionnaire candidates should welcome, complementing the warning in Principle 4 about pro-life questionnaires designed to extract compromise commitments.

related.title: Oklahomans for Life Asks Candidates to Oppose Abolition's Immediate Abolition
related.reason: Documents the exact pro-life questionnaire pressure the article warns candidates to ignore or refuse rather than sign.

related.title: Watch - Of Giants & Heroes: Joseph Silk
related.reason: Profiles Joseph Silk, the former state senator the article recommends as a coaching contact for campaign mechanics.
